name: camellia sinensis
mechanism: Caffeine antagonizes adenosine receptors and increases alertness. Catechins (including EGCG) have antioxidant and signaling effects in vitro and in vivo; clinical outcomes are plausible but depend on dose, matrix, and population.
effects: No direct effects data. Contextual inference:
nan.. No direct mechanismofaction data. Contextual inference:
contraindications: Use caution with prescription medications and chronic conditions; monitor for intolerance.
safetyNotes: Brewed tea is generally safe for most adults in moderate intake, but excess caffeine can cause insomnia, palpitations, anxiety, and GI symptoms. Concentrated green tea extracts have been linked to rare but potentially serious liver injury; stop use and seek care for jaundice, dark urine, or persistent abdominal symptoms.
description: Green, black, and oolong teas all derive from Camellia sinensis and differ mainly by processing. Human evidence supports short-term alertness effects from caffeine and potential cardiometabolic benefits from long-term tea intake patterns, but effect sizes vary and supplement extracts are not equivalent to brewed tea.
dosage: No single universal dose for all uses. Moderate brewed tea intake is the best-studied pattern; high-dose extract products require cautious use and label adherence.

interactions: Caffeine-containing tea products can interact with stimulant and cardiovascular medications, and may alter exposure to selected drugs.
NCCIH specifically notes interaction concerns between green tea and nadolol, and possible interactions with atorvastatin and raloxifene.
Separate high-dose extracts from complex medication regimens unless reviewed by a clinician.
preparation: Prepared mainly as brewed leaf tea; supplements include concentrated extracts and decaffeinated catechin products with different safety profiles.
region: Native to East and South Asia; now cultivated globally in tropical and subtropical tea-growing regions.
therapeuticUses: traditional support for mild digestive comfort
sideEffects: mild gastrointestinal upset (nausea, cramping, or loose stool)
allergic rash or itching in sensitive individuals
summary: Camellia sinensis (tea plant) provides caffeine and catechins; tea consumption is widely used for alertness and cardiometabolic support, while concentrated extracts require greater safety caution.
